title: Reduced GCS — Approach
oneLiner: A drop in Glasgow Coma Scale (<15) signals acute brain dysfunction — must rule out reversible metabolic/toxin causes and structural emergencies within minutes.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Hypoglycaemia (BG <3.0 mmol/L)** – single finding: rapid fingerstick glucose <3.0 mmol/L; treat immediately before any imaging.
- **Opioid overdose** – single finding: pinpoint pupils + respiratory rate <8/min + opioid history/risk; give naloxone urgently.
- **Intracranial haemorrhage or large stroke** – single finding: anisocoria (unequal pupils) OR focal neurological deficit on cursory exam; need urgent CT head.
- **Seizures (ongoing or post-ictal)** – single finding: rhythmic limb/facial jerking OR tongue bite + incontinence; treat with benzodiazepine, then CT if first seizure.
sections.Presentation:
- Reduced consciousness – drowsy, difficult to rouse, or unresponsive to voice/pain  
- Confusion, disorientation, or agitation preceding GCS drop  
- Abnormal motor response – localising, withdrawing, or none  
- Possible incontinence, tongue bite, or myoclonus (seizure)  
- Pupillary changes – size, symmetry, reactivity  
- Respiratory pattern – hyperventilation (metabolic acidosis), Cheyne-Stokes (bi-hemispheric), or irregular (brainstem)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Metabolic / toxic** – most common (15-75% of cases) – normal pupils, no focal signs, ± tremor/asterixis, bilaterally symmetrical  
  - Hypoglycaemia, uraemia, hepatic encephalopathy, hyponatraemia  
  - Alcohol intoxication (smell, nystagmus), benzodiazepines, opioids, antipsychotics, carbon monoxide  
- **Structural** – less common but high mortality – focal signs, anisocoria, progressive worsening  
  - Stroke (ischaemic or haemorrhagic), subarachnoid haemorrhage, subdural haematoma, tumour, hydrocephalus  
- **Infectious** – fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, rash  
  - Meningitis, encephalitis, cerebral abscess  
- **Seizure-related** – post-ictal drowsiness, tongue bite, incontinence, upgoing Babinski  
- **Psychiatric** – conversion disorder (pseudoseizure, catatonia) – normal pupils, inconsistent exam, no injury  

**Splitter:** *Pupil examination* – **equal and reactive** → strongly favours metabolic/toxic cause (or post-ictal); **unequal or fixed** → structural lesion until proven otherwise.
sections.Investigations:
**Bedside (first hour):**  
- Fingerstick glucose (mandatory, within seconds)  
- A–E assessment – Airway patency, Breathing (SaO₂, RR), Circulation (BP, HR, peripheral perfusion)  
- Pupil size and reactivity  
- ECG (rate, rhythm, signs of overdose – wide QRS, prolonged QT)  
- Check temperature (fever/hypothermia)  
- Look for evidence of trauma (scalp, bruising, Battle’s sign)  
- Brief neurological exam – motor response, eye opening, verbal response  

**Bloods:**  
- FBC, U&E, LFT, glucose, CRP, coagulation  
- Arterial or venous blood gas – pH, bicarbonate, lactate, pCO₂, glucose  
- Tox screen – paracetamol, salicylate, alcohol, urine drug screen  
- Blood cultures if febrile  
- Serum osmolality if suspect toxic alcohol  

**Imaging:**  
- **First-line:** Non-contrast CT head – must be performed **stat** in all new-onset reduced GCS with no clear reversible cause (NICE CG176)  
- **Second-line:** CT angiography (if subarachnoid haemorrhage suspected), MRI brain (if encephalitis, posterior fossa, or demyelination suspected)  

**Special tests:**  
- Lumbar puncture – after negative CT, if meningitis/encephalitis suspected  
- EEG – if non-convulsive status epilepticus suspected (prolonged confusion with fluctuating GCS)  

**Never-miss test:**  
- **Fingerstick blood glucose** – omission is a serious error: treat hypoglycaemia immediately, before any imaging or IV access.
sections.Management:
**Immediate (first hour / ward bay):**  
- A–E: protect airway if GCS ≤8 (call anaesthetics/ICU), give O₂ to target SpO₂ ≥94%, secure IV access  
- Treat hypoglycaemia: **50 mL 50% dextrose IV** (or 1 mg glucagon IM if no IV) – but first give **IV thiamine** (Pabrinex [check local policy]) in at-risk patients to prevent Wernicke’s encephalopathy  
- Suspected opioid overdose: **naloxone 0.4–2 mg IV** repeated every 2–3 min as needed (max 10 mg) – watch for acute withdrawal  
- Suspected seizure: **lorazepam 4 mg IV** (or diazepam 10 mg PR) – if status epilepticus, follow local status protocol  
- Suspected raised ICP: elevate head 30°, nurse flat if hypotensive; mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV or hypertonic saline [check local policy]  
- **Call senior immediately** – if GCS ≤8, seizure, focal signs, or NEWS2 ≥6  

**Ward (first 24–72h):**  
- Ongoing GCS monitoring – document every 15 min initially, then hourly  
- Repeat bloods (including toxicology) as directed by cause  
- CT head if not yet done, or CT angiogram if SAH/clot suspected  
- Manage underlying condition – antibiotics for meningitis (ceftriaxone + dexamethasone [check policy]), IV fluids for sepsis, reversal of anticoagulation if ICH, etc.  
- Neurosurgical referral if mass effect, ICH, or hydrocephalus  

**Long-term / outpatient:**  
- Rehabilitation if hypoxic brain injury or stroke  
- Seizure prophylaxis if indicated (not routinely)  
- Cognitive assessment and follow-up  

**Default Orders (if admitted):**  
- **Fluids:** 0.9% saline at 80–125 mL/h (avoid dextrose in first 24h unless hypoglycaemic); titrate to urine output and sodium  
- **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH once stable (no intracranial bleed; check coagulation before dose)  
- **Diet/NBM status:** NBM until GCS >14 or swallow assessment safe; if prolonged, consider NG feeding after airway secured  
- **Observation:** GCS + pupil check – 15 min for first 2 hours → hourly for 12 hours → 2–4 hourly thereafter (per local protocol)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Dextrose 50%** – 50 mL IV (25 g) over 3–5 min; repeat if no response  
- **Thiamine (Pabrinex)** – 1 pair of ampoules (IV) once before dextrose in at-risk (alcohol misuse, malnutrition) – [check local policy]  
- **Naloxone** – 0.4–2 mg IV initially, repeat every 2–3 min up to 10 mg; can also give IM  
- **Lorazepam** – 4 mg IV slow (or 10 mg PR diazepam) – max 8 mg IV within 10 min; watch for respiratory depression  
- **Ceftriaxone** – 2 g IV (empiric meningitis dose) – once daily (adjust if eGFR <10)  
- **Mannitol 20%** – 0.5–1 g/kg IV over 30 min (if raised ICP) – [check local policy]  
- **Key contraindication:** dextrose without thiamine in alcohol misuse → precipitates Wernicke’s encephalopathy  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** mannitol avoided in anuria; ceftriaxone halved if eGFR <10; lorazepam increased risk of encephalopathy in liver disease  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving dextrose 50% without first giving thiamine** in an alcoholic patient (risk of acute Wernicke’s) – always check history and co-administer Pabrinex.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** airway compromise leading to aspiration / respiratory arrest; raised ICP → coning; status epilepticus; hypoglycaemia recurrence; cardiac arrest from untreated opioid overdose  
- **Chronic:** hypoxic brain injury; persistent vegetative state; dementia after repeated episodes (e.g., alcoholic encephalopathy); post-ictal Todd’s paresis (resolves within 24h)
sections.Prognosis:
- Excellent if reversible cause (hypoglycaemia, opioid overdose, electrolyte disturbance) and early treatment – full recovery within minutes to hours  
- Poor outcome if GCS ≤8 for >6 hours, bilateral fixed pupils, absent brainstem reflexes, or significant structural injury (ICH, stroke) – 30-day mortality >50% in severe ICH
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Check blood glucose before calling for help** – it’s the quickest reversible killer  
- **Do a quick pupil exam** – it’s the best bedside splitter between metabolic and structural causes  
- **Give naloxone empirically** if you see pinpoint pupils + slow breathing – even if no clear history; it won’t harm and may save life  
- **Document GCS fully** – record eye, verbal, motor scores separately, not just total – it helps trending and neurosurgery referral
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don’t forget to give thiamine before dextrose** in at-risk patients – Wernicke’s is irreversible  
- **Don’t assume it’s “just post-ictal”** without ruling out hypoglycaemia, intracranial bleed, or meningitis  
- **Don’t delay CT head** for a long exam – if GCS ≤14 and unexplained, CT is mandatory  
- **Don’t give naloxone and then discharge the patient** after opioid overdose without 4–6h observation (naloxone half-life < opioid half-life, may re-sedate)
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - GCS drops again, even transiently  
  - New headache, vomiting, or focal weakness  
  - Seizure, confusion, or difficulty waking from sleep  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - GP review within 24–48h if metabolic/toxic cause  
  - Neurology or neurosurgery clinic if structural cause found on CT
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (SpR/Reg) immediately if:**  
  - GCS ≤8 (airway risk)  
  - Sudden drop of ≥2 points  
  - New anisocoria or fixed pupil  
  - Seizure lasting >5 min or without recovery  
  - NEWS2 ≥6 or lactate >4 mmol/L  
  - Deteriorating despite initial treatment  
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**  
  - GCS ≤8 with absent gag/cough or gasping respirations  
  - Requires immediate endotracheal intubation  
  - Cardiorespiratory arrest – initiate ALS  

---

**Reference:**  
- NICE CG176 (Head injury: assessment and early management)  
- NICE CG 127 (Meningitis)  
- RCUK Adult ALS 2021  
- BNF for drug doses; [check local policy] for specific thresholds and protocols in your trust.

rawMarkdown:
# Reduced GCS — Approach

**One-liner:**  
A drop in Glasgow Coma Scale (<15) signals acute brain dysfunction — must rule out reversible metabolic/toxin causes and structural emergencies within minutes.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Hypoglycaemia (BG <3.0 mmol/L)** – single finding: rapid fingerstick glucose <3.0 mmol/L; treat immediately before any imaging.
- **Opioid overdose** – single finding: pinpoint pupils + respiratory rate <8/min + opioid history/risk; give naloxone urgently.
- **Intracranial haemorrhage or large stroke** – single finding: anisocoria (unequal pupils) OR focal neurological deficit on cursory exam; need urgent CT head.
- **Seizures (ongoing or post-ictal)** – single finding: rhythmic limb/facial jerking OR tongue bite + incontinence; treat with benzodiazepine, then CT if first seizure.

### Presentation

- Reduced consciousness – drowsy, difficult to rouse, or unresponsive to voice/pain  
- Confusion, disorientation, or agitation preceding GCS drop  
- Abnormal motor response – localising, withdrawing, or none  
- Possible incontinence, tongue bite, or myoclonus (seizure)  
- Pupillary changes – size, symmetry, reactivity  
- Respiratory pattern – hyperventilation (metabolic acidosis), Cheyne-Stokes (bi-hemispheric), or irregular (brainstem)

### Differential Diagnosis

- **Metabolic / toxic** – most common (15-75% of cases) – normal pupils, no focal signs, ± tremor/asterixis, bilaterally symmetrical  
  - Hypoglycaemia, uraemia, hepatic encephalopathy, hyponatraemia  
  - Alcohol intoxication (smell, nystagmus), benzodiazepines, opioids, antipsychotics, carbon monoxide  
- **Structural** – less common but high mortality – focal signs, anisocoria, progressive worsening  
  - Stroke (ischaemic or haemorrhagic), subarachnoid haemorrhage, subdural haematoma, tumour, hydrocephalus  
- **Infectious** – fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, rash  
  - Meningitis, encephalitis, cerebral abscess  
- **Seizure-related** – post-ictal drowsiness, tongue bite, incontinence, upgoing Babinski  
- **Psychiatric** – conversion disorder (pseudoseizure, catatonia) – normal pupils, inconsistent exam, no injury  

**Splitter:** *Pupil examination* – **equal and reactive** → strongly favours metabolic/toxic cause (or post-ictal); **unequal or fixed** → structural lesion until proven otherwise.

### Investigations

**Bedside (first hour):**  
- Fingerstick glucose (mandatory, within seconds)  
- A–E assessment – Airway patency, Breathing (SaO₂, RR), Circulation (BP, HR, peripheral perfusion)  
- Pupil size and reactivity  
- ECG (rate, rhythm, signs of overdose – wide QRS, prolonged QT)  
- Check temperature (fever/hypothermia)  
- Look for evidence of trauma (scalp, bruising, Battle’s sign)  
- Brief neurological exam – motor response, eye opening, verbal response  

**Bloods:**  
- FBC, U&E, LFT, glucose, CRP, coagulation  
- Arterial or venous blood gas – pH, bicarbonate, lactate, pCO₂, glucose  
- Tox screen – paracetamol, salicylate, alcohol, urine drug screen  
- Blood cultures if febrile  
- Serum osmolality if suspect toxic alcohol  

**Imaging:**  
- **First-line:** Non-contrast CT head – must be performed **stat** in all new-onset reduced GCS with no clear reversible cause (NICE CG176)  
- **Second-line:** CT angiography (if subarachnoid haemorrhage suspected), MRI brain (if encephalitis, posterior fossa, or demyelination suspected)  

**Special tests:**  
- Lumbar puncture – after negative CT, if meningitis/encephalitis suspected  
- EEG – if non-convulsive status epilepticus suspected (prolonged confusion with fluctuating GCS)  

**Never-miss test:**  
- **Fingerstick blood glucose** – omission is a serious error: treat hypoglycaemia immediately, before any imaging or IV access.

### Management

**Immediate (first hour / ward bay):**  
- A–E: protect airway if GCS ≤8 (call anaesthetics/ICU), give O₂ to target SpO₂ ≥94%, secure IV access  
- Treat hypoglycaemia: **50 mL 50% dextrose IV** (or 1 mg glucagon IM if no IV) – but first give **IV thiamine** (Pabrinex [check local policy]) in at-risk patients to prevent Wernicke’s encephalopathy  
- Suspected opioid overdose: **naloxone 0.4–2 mg IV** repeated every 2–3 min as needed (max 10 mg) – watch for acute withdrawal  
- Suspected seizure: **lorazepam 4 mg IV** (or diazepam 10 mg PR) – if status epilepticus, follow local status protocol  
- Suspected raised ICP: elevate head 30°, nurse flat if hypotensive; mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV or hypertonic saline [check local policy]  
- **Call senior immediately** – if GCS ≤8, seizure, focal signs, or NEWS2 ≥6  

**Ward (first 24–72h):**  
- Ongoing GCS monitoring – document every 15 min initially, then hourly  
- Repeat bloods (including toxicology) as directed by cause  
- CT head if not yet done, or CT angiogram if SAH/clot suspected  
- Manage underlying condition – antibiotics for meningitis (ceftriaxone + dexamethasone [check policy]), IV fluids for sepsis, reversal of anticoagulation if ICH, etc.  
- Neurosurgical referral if mass effect, ICH, or hydrocephalus  

**Long-term / outpatient:**  
- Rehabilitation if hypoxic brain injury or stroke  
- Seizure prophylaxis if indicated (not routinely)  
- Cognitive assessment and follow-up  

**Default Orders (if admitted):**  
- **Fluids:** 0.9% saline at 80–125 mL/h (avoid dextrose in first 24h unless hypoglycaemic); titrate to urine output and sodium  
- **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH once stable (no intracranial bleed; check coagulation before dose)  
- **Diet/NBM status:** NBM until GCS >14 or swallow assessment safe; if prolonged, consider NG feeding after airway secured  
- **Observation:** GCS + pupil check – 15 min for first 2 hours → hourly for 12 hours → 2–4 hourly thereafter (per local protocol)  

### Drugs & Doses
- **Dextrose 50%** – 50 mL IV (25 g) over 3–5 min; repeat if no response  
- **Thiamine (Pabrinex)** – 1 pair of ampoules (IV) once before dextrose in at-risk (alcohol misuse, malnutrition) – [check local policy]  
- **Naloxone** – 0.4–2 mg IV initially, repeat every 2–3 min up to 10 mg; can also give IM  
- **Lorazepam** – 4 mg IV slow (or 10 mg PR diazepam) – max 8 mg IV within 10 min; watch for respiratory depression  
- **Ceftriaxone** – 2 g IV (empiric meningitis dose) – once daily (adjust if eGFR <10)  
- **Mannitol 20%** – 0.5–1 g/kg IV over 30 min (if raised ICP) – [check local policy]  
- **Key contraindication:** dextrose without thiamine in alcohol misuse → precipitates Wernicke’s encephalopathy  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** mannitol avoided in anuria; ceftriaxone halved if eGFR <10; lorazepam increased risk of encephalopathy in liver disease  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving dextrose 50% without first giving thiamine** in an alcoholic patient (risk of acute Wernicke’s) – always check history and co-administer Pabrinex.

### Complications
- **Acute:** airway compromise leading to aspiration / respiratory arrest; raised ICP → coning; status epilepticus; hypoglycaemia recurrence; cardiac arrest from untreated opioid overdose  
- **Chronic:** hypoxic brain injury; persistent vegetative state; dementia after repeated episodes (e.g., alcoholic encephalopathy); post-ictal Todd’s paresis (resolves within 24h)

### Prognosis
- Excellent if reversible cause (hypoglycaemia, opioid overdose, electrolyte disturbance) and early treatment – full recovery within minutes to hours  
- Poor outcome if GCS ≤8 for >6 hours, bilateral fixed pupils, absent brainstem reflexes, or significant structural injury (ICH, stroke) – 30-day mortality >50% in severe ICH

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Check blood glucose before calling for help** – it’s the quickest reversible killer  
- **Do a quick pupil exam** – it’s the best bedside splitter between metabolic and structural causes  
- **Give naloxone empirically** if you see pinpoint pupils + slow breathing – even if no clear history; it won’t harm and may save life  
- **Document GCS fully** – record eye, verbal, motor scores separately, not just total – it helps trending and neurosurgery referral

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don’t forget to give thiamine before dextrose** in at-risk patients – Wernicke’s is irreversible  
- **Don’t assume it’s “just post-ictal”** without ruling out hypoglycaemia, intracranial bleed, or meningitis  
- **Don’t delay CT head** for a long exam – if GCS ≤14 and unexplained, CT is mandatory  
- **Don’t give naloxone and then discharge the patient** after opioid overdose without 4–6h observation (naloxone half-life < opioid half-life, may re-sedate)

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - GCS drops again, even transiently  
  - New headache, vomiting, or focal weakness  
  - Seizure, confusion, or difficulty waking from sleep  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - GP review within 24–48h if metabolic/toxic cause  
  - Neurology or neurosurgery clinic if structural cause found on CT  

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (SpR/Reg) immediately if:**  
  - GCS ≤8 (airway risk)  
  - Sudden drop of ≥2 points  
  - New anisocoria or fixed pupil  
  - Seizure lasting >5 min or without recovery  
  - NEWS2 ≥6 or lactate >4 mmol/L  
  - Deteriorating despite initial treatment  
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**  
  - GCS ≤8 with absent gag/cough or gasping respirations  
  - Requires immediate endotracheal intubation  
  - Cardiorespiratory arrest – initiate ALS  

---

**Reference:**  
- NICE CG176 (Head injury: assessment and early management)  
- NICE CG 127 (Meningitis)  
- RCUK Adult ALS 2021  
- BNF for drug doses; [check local policy] for specific thresholds and protocols in your trust.
